content.full_text:
apta American Physical Therapy Association
1111 North Fairfax Street, Alexandria, Virginia 22314-1488 (703) 684-2782 • FAX (703) 684-7343

October 30, 1990

California Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807
ATTN: Lynda Cassady

Dear Ms. Cassady:

The American Physical Therapy Association (APTA) requests advice from the
California Fair Political Practices Commission concerning provisions of the
California Political Reform Act:

FACTS

The APTA is a national membership organization with Chapters in every
State. All members of the APTA in California are also members of the
California Chapter - APTA.

The APTA invoices its members in California not only for their dues in the
national organization but also for their dues in the California Chapter -
APTA. We are considering the incorporation of a line item on that dues
invoice which would enable the members of the California Chapter - APTA to
voluntarily include with their dues payments an additional amount of money
specifically designated as a contribution to CAL-PT-PAC. CAL-PT-PAC is the
sponsored committee of the California Chapter - APTA. The APTA would pass
these contributions along to CAL-PT-PAC.

QUESTIONS

1. Under these circumstances, would the APTA be considered as an
   intermediary or as the agent of CAL-PT-PAC, and what, if anything, is
   the difference between these two designations?

2. In addition to the names and addresses of each person who contributes
   to CAL-PT-PAC through the APTA, would the APTA also be required to
   provide CAL-PT-PAC with the names of contributors' employers, or is
   this responsibility that CAL-PT-PAC can legally assume on its own
   once contributions are reported by APTA to CAL-PT-PAC?

APTA
ORLANDO

JANUARY 31-FEBRUARY 3, 1991

Lynda Cassady
October 30, 1990
Page 2


3.     For reporting purposes, is the date of receipt of a contribution the
       day that contribution arrives at the APTA's lock box and is deposited
       in the APTA's account, or is it several days later when the APTA
       actually becomes aware that such dues payments were received in the
       lock box and deposited?

4.     With regard to contributions received on or near the end of a
       reporting period during an election year, would the APTA be required
       to report such contributions to CAL-PT-PAC no later than the closing
       date of that reporting period or would the APTA have until the filing
       date to report such contributions to CAL-PT-PAC?

5.     If the APTA has not learned of the receipt of a contribution to CAL-
       PT-PAC on the closing date of the reporting period and provides that
       information to CAL-PT-PAC at a later date, it is our understanding
       that the CAL-PT-PAC would disclose this information by filing an
       amended report.  Would any liability arise because an amended report
       had to be filed for that reason?

6.     Under these circumstances, what would be the advantage of the APTA
       being a sponsor of CAL-PT-PAC along with the California Chapter -
       APTA?

7.     Is it possible for the Commission to provide us with examples of
       other national associations and their California
       affiliates in a manner similar to that described in our fact
       situation?

Your responses to the above questions will be greatly appreciated.

Sincerely,

[signature]

R. Charles Harker, Esq.
Director of Government Affairs

sections.question:
1. Under these circumstances, would the APTA be considered as an intermediary or as the agent of CAL-PT-PAC, and what, if anything, is the difference between these two designations?

2. In addition to the names and addresses of each person who contributes to CAL-PT-PAC through the APTA, would the APTA also be required to provide CAL-PT-PAC with the names of contributors' employers, or is this responsibility that CAL-PT-PAC can legally assume on its own once contributions are reported by APTA to CAL-PT-PAC?

3. For reporting purposes, is the date of receipt of a contribution the day that contribution arrives at the APTA's lock box and is deposited in the APTA's account, or is it several days later when the APTA actually becomes aware that such dues payments were received in the lock box and deposited?

4. With regard to contributions received on or near the end of a reporting period during an election year, would the APTA be required to report such contributions to CAL-PT-PAC no later than the closing date of that reporting period or would the APTA have until the filing date to report such contributions to CAL-PT-PAC?

5. If the APTA has not learned of the receipt of a contribution to CAL-PT-PAC on the closing date of the reporting period and provides that information to CAL-PT-PAC at a later date, it is our understanding that the CAL-PT-PAC would disclose this information by filing an amended report. Would any liability arise because an amended report had to be filed for that reason?

6. Under these circumstances, what would be the advantage of the APTA being a sponsor of CAL-PT-PAC along with the California Chapter - APTA?

7. Is it possible for the Commission to provide us with examples of other national associations which function for their California affiliates in a manner similar to that described in our fact situation?

Your responses to the above questions will be greatly appreciated.

CAL-PT-PAC is the sponsored committee of the California Chapter - APTA. The APTA would pass these contributions along to CAL-PT-PAC.
